name: Under Armour, Inc.

profile.description: Under Armour, Inc. designs, develops, markets, and distributes performance apparel, footwear, and accessories for men, women, and youth globally. The company is executing a multi-year brand repositioning under founder CEO Kevin Plank, who returned in April 2024 to lead a turnaround focused on premium brand elevation, channel cleanup, and cost restructuring after consecutive years of revenue decline and net losses. Headquartered in Baltimore, Maryland.

innovation.label: WEAK
innovation.analysis: Track Record (0/3): Under Armour has not launched a successful new product category or meaningful business model transformation in the past five years — the company has been in defensive restructuring mode, contracting revenue from $5.9B (FY2023) to $5.0B (FY2026) and exiting unprofitable channels rather than innovating [1]. AI Deployment (1/2): UA hired Liz Bacelar from Estee Lauder as head of AI and advanced analytics, building a team of data scientists and AI specialists to integrate generative AI into operations; the Connected Fitness platform (MapMyFitness) provides a digital foundation [2]. Market Share (1/2): Under Armour is a meaningful player in performance athletic wear but has been losing share to Nike and Adidas — apparel revenue declined 2% and footwear revenue fell 11% in FY2026, indicating share erosion rather than gains [1]. Product Pipeline (1/2): FY2027 guidance signals continued slight revenue decline; no specific breakthrough product launches have been announced with defined timelines, though Plank has discussed refocusing on performance innovation. R&D Intensity (0/1): R&D spending is below Nike and Adidas as a percentage of revenue.

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): Under Armour's mission — 'to make all athletes better through passion, design, and the relentless pursuit of innovation' — is specific, distinctive, and genuinely connects the company's work to athletic performance identity [3]. It clearly differentiates from generic corporate statements and has guided the brand since Kevin Plank's founding vision. However, the mission has not been consistently lived out through years of drift and restructuring. Community Impact (0/2): No evidence of distinctive charitable programs or community investment beyond standard corporate sponsorships and athlete partnerships. Employee Retention (0/2): Multiple waves of restructuring, CEO changes (Plank to Frisk back to Plank), and workforce reduction from 16,000 to 6,200 employees indicate significant organizational instability and low trust [2]. Insider Buying (1/2): Prem Watsa (Fairfax Financial, 10%+ beneficial owner) purchased $120M+ in UAA shares between December 2025 and May 2026, including 11.5M shares in December 2025 alone; Director Mohamed El-Erian bought $520K in August 2025 [4]. Meaningful conviction from major shareholders.

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored as growth stock. FY2026 (ended March 2026) delivered revenue of $5.0B (down 3.8% GAAP) with a net loss of $496M and adjusted net income of only $50M [1]. D/E of 137% is elevated (score 0.71) with $1.94B debt against $309M cash. ROE is deeply negative at -30% (score 0.0, 2x weight). Revenue growth YoY of -3.8% (score 0.54). 5-year revenue CAGR of -2.2% reflects sustained contraction (score 0.19, 2x weight). Forward P/E of 25.0 is above target (score 0.88). Profit margin is -10% on GAAP basis (score 0.0). PEG of 1.84 is moderate (score 0.58, 2x weight). Weighted sum 3.66/10 = 4. Liquidity: $6.05 x 8.0M shares/day = $48M, passes floor.

ai_resilience.label: MODERATE
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (6/9): Under Armour's core business — designing, manufacturing, and marketing physical athletic apparel and footwear — involves tangible products and brand identity that AI cannot replace [1]. The physical supply chain, retail presence, and athlete endorsement relationships create structural moats against AI substitution. However, AI poses meaningful competitive threats: Nike's 190M+ Nike app users create superior AI-driven personalization capabilities, generative AI tools could democratize apparel design reducing UA's creative differentiation, and AI-powered direct-to-consumer platforms from competitors threaten UA's wholesale-dependent distribution model [2]. Analysts note retailers risk losing data and direct consumer interaction as shoppers migrate to AI shopping platforms. AI Opportunity (0/1): Despite hiring an AI head from Estee Lauder, UA has not yet generated quantifiable AI revenue — the Connected Fitness platform and AI analytics initiatives are pre-revenue from an AI perspective [2].

infinite_game.summary: Under Armour's return of founder Kevin Plank as CEO in April 2024 represents an attempt to reconnect the company with its founding athletic performance identity — a potentially infinite-game restoration. However, the company has been in reactive restructuring for five years with massive workforce reductions (16,000 to 6,200), consecutive revenue declines, and a $496M net loss in FY2026. Plank's public praise of micromanagement as 'totally underestimated' in business suggests a command-and-control leadership style that conflicts with Sinek's trust-based organizational model [2].

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): Under Armour's original cause — making athletes better through performance innovation — is genuine, specific, and connected to a larger purpose. Plank's return narrative explicitly centers on restoring this founding identity after years of brand dilution through over-expansion and off-price distribution [2][3]. The mission clearly differentiates from competitors and inspires loyalty among core athletes. However, the cause has not been consistently lived out — years of financial engineering, channel stuffing, and leadership instability undermined it, and it hasn't been tested through multi-decade consistency.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Plank's willingness to accept consecutive years of revenue contraction (FY2025 -9.4%, FY2026 -3.8%, FY2027 guidance further decline) in service of rebuilding brand equity demonstrates some courageous long-term thinking over short-term revenue defense [1]. Exiting profitable but brand-dilutive off-price channels is a sacrifice of near-term revenue for long-term positioning. However, these decisions were arguably forced by years of mismanagement rather than proactive courage.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): Massive workforce reduction from 16,000 to 6,200 employees, multiple CEO transitions, and ongoing leadership changes signal a low-trust environment [2]. Plank's public endorsement of micromanagement as 'totally underestimated' directly contradicts Sinek's Circle of Safety philosophy [2]. Frequent executive turnover — including recent elevation of Americas president to chief merchant and other leadership reshuffles — creates organizational instability incompatible with trusting teams.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (0/2): Under Armour has not demonstrated transformative strategic flexibility — the company is returning to its original athletic performance roots rather than innovating toward new markets or business models. The restructuring represents defensive retrenchment, not a visionary pivot driven by a Just Cause [1]. The company fundamentally sells the same products through the same channels as it did a decade ago, just with fewer employees and less revenue.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): Under Armour explicitly acknowledges Nike and Adidas as rivals whose strengths expose UA's weaknesses in brand storytelling, global distribution, digital ecosystem, and footwear innovation [2]. Plank's return narrative is framed around learning from what these competitors did right and using their strengths as motivation for UA's improvement rather than just trying to beat them on market share.

investment_thesis: Under Armour's bull case is a founder-led turnaround with meaningful insider conviction: Prem Watsa's Fairfax Financial acquired $120M+ in UAA shares since December 2025, and Director Mohamed El-Erian added $520K in August 2025, signaling deep-value confidence [4]. At $6.05/share ($2.6B market cap), the stock trades near liquidation levels for a brand with 47.9% gross margins and global recognition. Plank's channel cleanup — exiting off-price and low-margin wholesale — is painful but positions the brand for higher-quality, higher-margin revenue if the turnaround succeeds. FY2027 guidance of $96-116M operating income would represent a return to profitability from FY2026's $496M net loss [1].
thesis_continued: The bear case is that Under Armour's fundamental competitive position continues to deteriorate. Revenue has declined for three consecutive fiscal years (-9.4%, -3.8%, and guided further decline in FY2027) while Nike and Lululemon continue to take share with superior brand execution and digital ecosystems [1]. The $496M FY2026 net loss, negative ROE of -30%, D/E of 137%, and negative free cash flow of -$528M indicate fragile financial health that limits strategic flexibility. The workforce reduction from 16,000 to 6,200 and Plank's embrace of micromanagement risk creating a talent drain at exactly the moment the company needs its best people. Any tariff escalation on Asian-sourced manufacturing would further compress the gross margin that is currently the only bright spot in the financials.
key_catalysts: Return to GAAP profitability in FY2027 with guided operating income of $96-116M, proving the restructuring is delivering sustainable results beyond cost-cutting
North American revenue stabilization and return to growth demonstrating that the channel cleanup and brand elevation are working — North America is the core market where UA must win
Successful footwear product launch capturing meaningful market share — footwear declined 11% in FY2026 and remains Under Armour's weakest segment versus Nike and Adidas
key_risks: Consecutive years of revenue decline (FY2024-FY2027) could create a death spiral where shrinking revenue forces further cost cuts, driving away talent and eroding the brand's relevance among athletes
D/E of 137% and negative free cash flow of -$528M leave minimal financial cushion — any further deterioration could force dilutive equity raises or asset sales at distressed valuations
Kevin Plank's micromanagement philosophy and revolving executive leadership create organizational instability that undermines the multi-year turnaround timeline required for brand restoration
sources: Under Armour FY2026 (ended March 2026) Full Year and Q4 Results — SEC Form 8-K and StockTitan, May 2026
Under Armour CEO Leadership, AI Hiring, and Culture Coverage — Retail Dive, Fortune, Adweek, PR Newswire, 2025-2026
